era_context: Turn of the 14th century: The Late Medieval period in Europe; the Yuan Dynasty rules China under Kublai Khan's successors; the Ottoman Empire is in its infancy in Anatolia; the Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; the Mali Empire is rising in West Africa; the Renaissance is beginning to emerge in Italy.

events.title: Jubilee Year proclaimed by Pope Boniface VIII
events.description: Pope Boniface VIII proclaimed the first Jubilee Year (Holy Year) in 1300, offering plenary indulgences to pilgrims who visited Rome. The Jubilee Year was a major religious and political event, drawing hundreds of thousands of pilgrims to Rome and generating significant revenue for the papacy. The proclamation demonstrated papal authority and the continued spiritual power of the Church, even as secular rulers were beginning to challenge papal temporal authority. The Jubilee Year became a recurring institution in the Catholic Church.

sources.name: Dante Alighieri, Divine Comedy (contemporary literary reference)

events.certainty_note: The proclamation of the Jubilee Year in 1300 is well-documented in papal records and contemporary sources, including Dante's Divine Comedy.

events.title: Dante Alighieri begins composition of the Divine Comedy
events.description: Dante Alighieri, an Italian poet and intellectual, began composition of the Divine Comedy around 1300. The Divine Comedy is one of the greatest works of medieval literature and a foundational text of the Italian language and European culture. The poem, written in vernacular Italian rather than Latin, represented a shift toward the use of native languages in high literature and contributed to the development of Italian national identity. The Divine Comedy also reflected contemporary political and religious conflicts, including the Jubilee Year and the conflict between papal and secular authority.
events.certainty_note: Dante began composition of the Divine Comedy around 1300, but the exact date is uncertain. Most scholars place the beginning of composition between 1308 and 1320, with 1300 being a traditional but approximate date.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Kublai Khan's successors
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty, founded by Kublai Khan, continued to rule China in 1300 under his successors. By 1300, the Yuan Dynasty was facing internal instability and pressure from Mongol factions competing for control. The dynasty would continue to decline over the next few decades, eventually being overthrown by the Ming Dynasty in 1368. In 1300, the Yuan Dynasty was still a major power but showing signs of the internal conflicts that would eventually lead to its collapse.
events.certainty_note: The Yuan Dynasty's rule over China in 1300 is well-documented in imperial records and Chinese histories.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate consolidates control over the Levant and Egypt
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate, having eliminated the last Crusader strongholds (Acre fell in 1291), consolidated control over Egypt and the Levant in 1300. The Mamluks established themselves as the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and maintained control over the holy cities of Mecca and Medina. The Mamluk Sultanate would remain a major power in the region until the Ottoman conquest in the 16th century.
events.certainty_note: Mamluk control over Egypt and the Levant in 1300 is well-documented in administrative records and chronicles.

events.title: Ottoman Empire in its infancy under Osman I
events.description: Osman I, founder of the Ottoman Empire, was consolidating power in northwestern Anatolia around 1300. The Ottoman state was still small and regional in scope, but Osman's military campaigns against Byzantine territories were beginning to establish the Ottomans as a significant regional power. Over the next century, the Ottomans would expand dramatically and eventually become one of the major powers of the Mediterranean and Eastern Europe.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman early history is poorly documented; the exact date of Osman I's rise to power is uncertain, traditionally placed around 1299–1300.

events.title: Mali Empire consolidates power in West Africa
events.description: The Mali Empire, having emerged as a major West African power in the 13th century, continued to consolidate control over the trans-Saharan trade routes and gold production in 1300. Mali's wealth and power were based on control of gold mines and trade networks connecting West Africa to North Africa and the Mediterranean. The Mali Empire would reach its peak under Mansa Musa (r. 1312–1337), whose famous hajj to Mecca in 1324 would bring Mali to the attention of the wider Islamic world.
events.certainty_note: Mali's consolidation of power in 1300 is attested in later Arab geographers and oral traditions, but contemporary documentation is sparse.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Dante's composition of the Divine Comedy is traditionally placed around 1300, but modern scholarship suggests he began composition later, between 1308 and 1320. The exact starting date of composition remains uncertain. The founding date of the Ottoman Empire under Osman I is traditionally placed around 1299–1300, but some sources place it later. No other significant disconfirming evidence identified for the remaining events.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1300 is moderate but unevenly distributed. European sources (papal, Italian, and English records) are abundant, particularly regarding the Jubilee Year and Dante. Islamic sources (Mamluk and Ilkhanate records) are substantial but often compiled later. Chinese sources exist but are filtered through Yuan imperial historiography. Ottoman sources are sparse and largely dependent on later compilations. West African sources are sparse and largely dependent on later Arab geographers and oral traditions. The record is heavily weighted toward political and cultural events in Europe; economic, technological, and social developments are underrepresented. The Jubilee Year and Dante's Divine Comedy are significant cultural and religious events but may overshadow equally important developments in Asia and Africa.

graph_edges.note: The Jubilee Year and Dante's composition of the Divine Comedy occurred in the same period and are thematically related.
Dante's Divine Comedy references the Jubilee Year and contemporary religious and political conflicts.
Mali's consolidation of power in 1300 enabled Mansa Musa's famous hajj in 1324.
